name: Leucothea
description: Leucothea, also known as Ino, is a sea goddess in Greek mythology. She was originally a mortal queen of Thebes, daughter of Cadmus and Harmonia, and sister of Semele, Agave, and Autonoe. Driven mad by Hera as punishment for Semele's affair with Zeus, Ino tragically murdered her son Melicertes. To escape the wrath of her husband Athamas, who was also driven mad, she leaped into the sea with Melicertes in her arms. The gods, taking pity on her, transformed her into a sea goddess, Leucothea, and her son into the sea god Palaemon. As Leucothea, she became a benevolent protector of sailors in distress, offering assistance and guidance. She is often depicted as a woman emerging from the waves, offering a veil or scarf to shipwrecked sailors, promising safe passage. Her powers include control over the sea, the ability to calm storms, and the power to grant assistance and protection to those who call upon her in times of maritime peril. She is particularly known for her assistance to Odysseus during his journey home, offering him a veil that protected him from drowning after Poseidon wrecked his raft. Her transformation symbolizes redemption and the enduring power of maternal love even in the face of tragedy. She represents hope and salvation for those lost at sea, offering a beacon of light in the darkest of storms. Her worship was widespread in coastal regions of Greece, and she was often invoked by sailors before embarking on perilous voyages. Her story serves as a reminder of the capricious nature of the gods and the enduring strength of the human spirit.
subtitle: Sea goddess, protector of sailors

keyMyths.title: Ino's Madness and Transformation
keyMyths.description: Driven mad by Hera, Ino murders her son and leaps into the sea. The gods transform her into the sea goddess Leucothea.

keyMyths.title: Leucothea and Odysseus
keyMyths.description: Leucothea offers Odysseus a veil to protect him from drowning after Poseidon destroys his raft, enabling him to reach Scheria.

keyMyths.title: The Fate of Melicertes
keyMyths.description: Ino's son, Melicertes, is transformed into the sea god Palaemon after they both leap into the sea. He is worshipped in Corinth.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides the account of Leucothea aiding Odysseus.
sources.text: Provides the account of Leucothea aiding Odysseus.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Details the story of Ino's madness and transformation.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: Describes the worship of Palaemon and Ino's association with Corinth.
